text: Tyler Fisher: Hello, all right, start over for the stream. Hello, I'm Tyler Fisher. I
make a project called SIL, which is a client on top of the blue sky post lexicon that aggregates
the top links in your network. It's a unique client experience that nothing else in the
atmosphere offers right now. And I think we need more unique types of clients. So I'm
going to talk about how we can achieve that today. Last year, I moved to Harlem, a small
city in the Netherlands. At the center of the city, we have the Hrultenmarkt, a large public
plaza that is overlooked by the Sint Babelkerk, the giant church. It's a grand plaza that on
the weekends turns into a public square for local vendors. But at any time during the week,
it's a lovely place to be. It's quiet, safe, comfortable. You'll also notice in this photo,
it's car free. The Netherlands is famous for its pedestrian
and bicycle infrastructure. But it wasn't always this way. The Hrultenmarkt in the 1960s was
more of a parking lot and a road and a traffic circle going through it. When visitors visit
the Hrultenmarkt today, they tend to think of it as it's always been this way. This is
an ancient public square that's been this way since the 1300s. But it's not true. The Hrultenmarkt
was transformed as a result of sustained protest and community engagement in Harlem during the
1960s and 70s to rip out the roads, rip out the parking, and make it a pedestrianized plaza.
This got me thinking about how cities transform to places that are designed more for people and what
we can learn from walkable cities about transforming digital spaces. Ultimately, walkable cities are about
getting people to walk or take some other form of mobility to their destination instead of driving a
car. The American city planner, Jeff Speck, gave a talk in 2013 about building walkable cities. He says
that a walk has to offer four things for somebody to walk or take some other form of mobility
instead of drive. And I saw for these four things simultaneously. There needs to be a proper reason
to walk. The walk has to be safe and feel safe. The walk has to be comfortable. And the walk has to be
interesting. I, like many of you, have been thinking about how do we grow the atmosphere. Building the
atmosphere is an infrastructural change to the internet on the level of transforming a city from one that is
car-centric to one that is person-centric. So in thinking about this problem, I found myself revisiting
Speck's theory, but for the atmosphere. There needs to be a proper reason to join the atmosphere. The
atmosphere has to be safe and feel safe. The atmosphere has to be comfortable. And the atmosphere has to be
interesting. We can break this theory into four parts. Purpose, a proper reason to join the atmosphere.
Safety, the atmosphere has to be safe and feel safe. Comfort, the atmosphere has to be comfortable.
And interest, the atmosphere has to be interesting. I'd like to propose this four-component framing
as a structure for how we think about building atmospheric clients. In this talk, I'm going to walk
through each of these four components and what they mean for client design. I'll talk about how I think
about them in my own projects and offer some inspiration from elsewhere in the atmosphere that I think do some
of these things particularly well and discuss what opportunities I see for us to further atmospheric
clients in these four directions. Let's start with purpose. Speck's original theory describes us as
having a proper reason to walk. For this, he means having a destination in mind. For a walkable city,
you have to have schools and doctors, restaurants, bars, grocery stores, everything you need within walking
distance. We're all here building new apps, new experiences, trying to entice people to join the
open social web. But why would they? Often we lead with pitches about dated sovereignty, privacy, openness,
and while these are critical features, it's why we're all here. They should be a given for atmospheric
applications. People need more purpose than that. It's not enough to build the pedestrianized streets of the
internet. They have to go somewhere. The inability to convince people to join new social networks is
a long study problem. Switching costs are real. People won't move unless their network has already moved.
We can't solve this problem overnight. What's exciting about AT Proto is the opportunities offered by the
PDS. There have been over 40 million signups for Blue Sky so far, each with their own PDS. Each of those
already have an account with your app too. But if you're offering your own experience and your own
lexicon separate from the Blue Sky experience, this is somewhat cold comfort. 40 million people can sign
into your app, but if there is no real content and their people aren't on your lexicons yet,
there's not going to be much for them to engage with. So how can we leverage the Blue Sky existing
network, but offer new experiences that offer new motivations to join the atmosphere? One option is
to build clients on top of the existing lexicons, but offer new experiences. And that's what I've done
with Sill, my link aggregation client that is built on top of Blue Sky and Mastodon. Sill reads your
following timeline, finds the most popular links in your network, and offers you a different picture of
your feed by rank ordering the most popular links in your network. It's most common use case for people
is as a newsreader. Typically, the most commonly shared URLs are news stories. Many people use social
media for news, and Sill offers them an optimized way to do so. This is a new experience built on top of an
existing lexicon. But Sill's read-only. What if you wanted to offer a whole new social experience
without having to bootstrap a whole new lexicon? Sebastian Vogelsang's Flashes offers a really
compelling concept here. It's a photo sharing app akin to Instagram, but it uses the Blue Sky Post
lexicon as its root. But rather than limit itself to the limitations of the Post lexicon in terms of
photo sharing, it adds sidecar records on top of the Blue Sky Post so that in the Flashes app you get a
more full photo sharing experience. But Blue Sky users can still see and interact with those posts,
so you're immediately getting the benefit of being on the atmosphere while still offering a new
experience. So now the atmosphere as a whole has two separate experiences to offer people,
microblogging, and photo sharing, and they're seamlessly connected, letting everyone take advantage of the
increasingly growing social graph. As the atmosphere grows, more lexicons will get adopted,
offering more opportunities for extension, remixing, and combination from the client experience.
The community developing around standard outside is bearing a ton of fruit here.
In the long form space, I would love to see more work around premium news reading experiences.
There's both a supply and demand problem here of getting news onto protocol and getting readers
to understand that there is news on the protocol. I'm going to talk more about news on ATProto at a
panel tomorrow at 10 a.m. if you want to hear me talk more about that specifically.
But back to Speck's theory, he says that Walk must be safe and feel safe. This phrasing is specific.
There are, in fact, two related things here: being safe and feeling safe. It's an important distinction
and one we have to think about as we design digital spaces. When you first talk about safety in digital
spaces, the first thing you're going to think of is content moderation. I have no personal experience
with content moderation. It's not something I do instill beyond allowing people to mute words and domains
and accounts. I'm just not an expert, so I'm not going to say much about content moderation today,
and I'd rather point you to the experts in the space. Black Sky just released Acorn, which is a community
moderation tool that they built with prioritizing the health of the moderators. And I think that's a
really awesome approach. The team at Roost has released Osprey, which is a more automated approach
to dealing with abuse on social media and automating that moderation burden. And then research by Aaron
Kassane and Darius Kazemi from a few years ago. They did research on moderation in the Fediverse and
composable moderation. It is about the Fediverse, but I think it offers a ton of lessons as we think
about this in the atmosphere as well. I want to tackle safety from a different angle, which is how can
our social media clients encourage healthier social media habits? After all, even in a well-moderated
network, we can become addicted. When the news is bad, we can fall prey to doom scrolling. Even with
reverse chronological timelines or algorithmic timelines, the context collapse of different topics
matched together can overwhelm and confuse us. With Sill, I want to find solutions to these problems.
By default, Sill aggregates to the top links in your network over 24 hours. So if you leave it at that
default, Sill doesn't actually change very much throughout the day. And this is actually a good
thing if your primary use case for social media is reading news. Your network will surface the news that
matters. And once it reaches you, there's going to be a ton of context from your network helping you
process it, helping you understand it. As the story develops, you don't need to continue scrolling your
timeline where only every tenth post is actually the thing you're trying to learn about right now.
Sill groups it all together, keeping your mind focused and allowing you to read the story and
grapple with its ramifications without jumping to another topic and switching contexts.
Sill's -- these tactics are working with Sill's users who regularly report that it's helping them
use social media less while still staying engaged. David Crespo says he stays logged out of Blue Sky on his
phone and only uses Sill on his phone. Robinson Meyer says he can dip in and out throughout the day.
David Ziff says if you want to use Blue Sky to follow the news but you find feeds overwhelming,
Sill is a good alternative to that. This is giving users a lever of control over their experience,
and this contributes to them feeling safe in the atmosphere.
What other modalities can we offer in the atmosphere that offer a more controlled experience over
social media? I really love Anasota, which turns Blue Sky into a card swipe-based experience and
layers on like a moth collecting game and all kinds of really neat artistic approaches to social media.
It also has similar motivations to Sill of calm and focus rather than the real-time drip feed of social
media, traditional social media design. It has a stamina meter, so if you swipe too much,
it tells you you got to stop, you're out of stamina. I think it's really brilliant and offers a ton of
things to learn. Overall, how else can we help users to leverage the collective intelligence of their
networks without forcing them into the same tired designs that we've been using for social media for
the last two decades? We've spent enough time with both chronological timelines and algorithmic timelines
to know the problems well, so it's time to move beyond these and offer new experiences
for people that give them control and help them feel safe in the atmosphere.
Back to our walkable cities analogy, Speck says the walk has to be comfortable.
In the physical space, that can mean quiet roads, accessible crosswalks, well-paid paths.
In the digital space, I think about comfort as meeting people where they are,
as joining the atmosphere should not require a downgrade to their user experience,
and this is a really tall task. We're going to have to stretch ourselves. Many of us,
myself included, are believers in the web over native apps, but it's undeniable that users prefer
and expect native apps, especially on their phones. With sale, the number one question I get when I tell
people less online than me about it is, how do I get it in the app store? It's a website. I say,
just put it in your web browser, sale.social. At that point, I lose a lot of people. They might try it
once while I'm there in the room with them. If I see them a week later, I ask them how it's going,
and they haven't gone back. They don't think about it. They're not in the web browser that way.
I'd show them how you can add it to your home screen. SIL is a progressive web app,
but after walking through that menu diving and explaining the differences between
progressive web apps and native apps, I've already lost them. They just want to be able to install
it from the app store. So I built an iOS app for SIL that's now in testing, and if you want to get on
the test flight, here's the QR code. I hope to build an Android app after I get through the launch
process at the iOS app. Over half of SIL's web traffic comes from iOS users, so that's why I started
with iOS. And begrudgingly, I actually do now see how a fully native app for SIL offers some real
benefits for those users. With deep OS integration, I can offer push notifications. This takes SIL's
notification feature that already existed to its natural conclusion, right? Now SIL can tell you,
based on parameters you define, SIL can send you a push notification when news is emerging in your
network, and it can find you when it happens. Sorry, I lost myself. It can offer widgets,
giving you a quick at-a-glance view of the top link in your network without ever having to really even
open the app. I can offer a native share target. SIL also does bookmarking. So with the native share
target, you can bookmark a link from anywhere you experience it within the operating system.
It is currently in beta, and you can, yeah, join the Tesla if you would like, and let me know how it
goes. I'd love your feedback. For all of big tech's faults and misalignments, they offer a
comfortable experience to users. They employ legions of people to focus exclusively on user experience,
and they do it on every platform. Web, iOS, Android, and more. Many of us in the atmosphere,
myself included, our founders, our solo founders, or small teams. Somehow we have to deliver on better
user experiences for people with far fewer resources. But here's our advantage. For all big tech's
resources, to throw at user experience, they often throw them at misaligned ends, misaligned with the
interest of the everyday user. If we as a community can stay aligned on centering user needs and furthering the vision that we all have of a better internet,
we can collectively offer a better experience than our competition. I'm inspired by the interoperability
story offered by Semble and Margin. Semble is a sense-making tool letting you collect collections of
bookmarks and blue sky posts and other things around the web. And you can organize them into various collections.
You can do that individually. You can do that in a public collection together with people in the atmosphere.
Margin is an annotation layer for the web where you add comments and annotations to any web page and save that
annotation to your PDS. They're working together so that you can -- your bookmarks from either Margin or Semble
both work within both tools as you're using them. So that allows Semble to focus on where its innovation
is in the shared collections and organizational tools. And Margin can focus on its innovation of
being able to annotate anything on the web. But they all work together and they make each other better.
And it's not a competitive experience. It's a collaborative experience.
We need more shared infrastructure in order to pull things off like this. Depending on your use case,
it could be really hard to mix and match lexicons in the atmosphere right now, especially if
one of the lexons you want to use is a blue sky one. Now you're talking about indexing terabytes
of data if you want a full network backfill. So I love things like the microcosm set of tools
offer a ton of really easy ways for people to jump in and pull different records from throughout the
atmosphere. I'd like to see more tools that offer more use cases. Something I'd really love to see
is a network-wide app view that's designed for aggregative workloads, by which I mean,
you know, group by the types of fundamentally database queries that SIL is doing, right? Grouping
by link, grouping by tangled star. You can find links within leaflet docs, standard site docs. I think it
opens up a ton of interesting opportunities for experiences that collect the whole atmosphere and not
just focus on one app experience. Our last component, interest, is maybe the most surprising when you think
about walkable cities for specs components. But for the atmosphere, I think it's probably the most
obvious one, right? We have to build into interesting things. We're competing with the whole internet for
everybody's time and we have to win. What sticks out to me when I think about making interesting things
around the atmosphere is they have to be interesting immediately. This is a place where I think the
client experience can do a lot of work, even if you're not interested in developing new lexicons.
But to do it right is going to take some coordinated effort. Let's use SIL as an example of what I mean.
With SIL, I've been thinking about how to onboard people into the atmosphere if they don't already have
a PDS. And I want to point them in the direction of trustworthy news that matches their needs and
interests. Today's SIL only really works if you've invested time in building your own network and your
following graph so that it is a trusted network of information that you'd like. For people in this room,
that's probably pretty easy to do. But for new users, that's a really tough hurdle to climb.
People need help finding people. And while it had some flaws, one of, I think, the key parts of
growth of Blue Sky's 2024 post-election growth was the starter pack. And it was received well enough
that the competition copied it. Meta has starter packs and threads. Blue Sky's other competitive edge
is custom fees and algorithmic choice. Combining these two things, we can bootstrap a user's network
based on their interests rather quickly. This is not live in SIL yet. I haven't started building.
But here's how I see a SIL possible onboarding experience working. Users without an atmosphere
account can register with a PDS. SIL doesn't need to run that PDS. I don't want to run a PDS. But I'm
open to partnering with anybody who is operating a PDS and thinks something like this would be interesting
to host new users. And I'd especially love if it wasn't Blue Sky so that we can further the
decentralization story of the atmosphere at the same time. After they register with the PDS,
they can come back to SIL and tell SIL a bit about what publications they read, what topics they're
interested in. And through a combination of starter packs lists and custom feeds, they can
immediately bootstrap their graph. And then SIL is ready and useful on day one as soon as they
finish that process. And they can go over to the Blue Sky app or whatever other client. And it's already set up
and ready to go. But maybe you see the problem with this idea. It requires maintaining a complex set of
feeds, lists, and starter packs. Keeping all those maintained over time as people leave and join the
network, it would become my full-time job if I was going to do that alone. I wouldn't be able to work on
the app. I would just work on maintaining feeds. So making sense of the Blue Sky Social Graph has many
uses for many applications. So this is something I think we could maybe take a collective approach to
and trying to find the bubbles that exist already. I know there's been some mapping projects that people
have done, and I wonder if we can build on top of those to automate some starter packs and some feeds
and point people in the right direction as soon as they get started. Sports, I think, offer a really
unique opportunity here. A lot of Blue Sky, my understanding is a lot of Blue Sky's post-2024
growth has come from sports. Usage tends to peak around major American sporting events like the World
Series and Super Bowl. But sports are also a place where X and other social networks maintain a strong
foothold. So how can we use community-driven feeds and starter packs to easily onboard people with the
already vibrant sports network that Blue Sky has? And how might we use those networks to encourage
activity and the atmosphere overall? I can imagine onboarding clients that ask you what's your favorite
team and then just setting up your graph immediately based on your favorite teams and favorite leagues.
Transforming the internet from the walled gardens of Web 2.0 to the openness of the
atmosphere promises is no smaller an effort than transforming a city from one centered around the

came about around the Olympics. While politically controversial at the time, Hidalgo spent 12 years in
office making changes that made Paris more walkable. This year, she chose not to run for a third term.
So Paris had the opportunity to maybe reset if they didn't like these changes. They elected Emmanuel

just four principles to round out the implementation: purpose, safety, comfort, and interest. Thank you.
